tools.description: Close a GitHub issue with a closing comment. Use this when work is complete, the issue is no longer relevant, or it's a duplicate. The closing comment should explain the resolution or reason for closing. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be closed. Target: *.

body.description: Closing comment explaining why the issue is being closed and summarizing any resolution, workaround, or conclusion.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to close. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 901 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/901). If omitted, closes the issue that triggered this workflow (requires an issue event trigger).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

state_reason.description: The reason for closing the issue. Use 'COMPLETED' for resolved issues, 'NOT_PLANNED' for issues that won't be addressed, or 'DUPLICATE' for duplicate issues. Defaults to 'COMPLETED'.

tools.name: close_issue

tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Target: *.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Add labels to an existing GitHub issue or pull request for categorization and filtering. Labels must already exist in the repository. For creating new issues with labels, use create_issue with the labels property instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be added. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to add labels to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, adds labels to the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to add (e.g., ['bug', 'priority-high']). Labels must exist in the repository. This field is required — omitting it will cause a validation error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_labels

tools.description: Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be removed. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to remove labels from. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: remove_labels

tools.description: Assign one or more GitHub users to an issue. Use this to delegate work to specific team members. Users must have access to the repository.

assignee.description: Single GitHub username to assign. Use 'assignees' array for multiple users.

assignees.description: GitHub usernames to assign to the issue (e.g., ['octocat', 'mona']). Users must have access to the repository.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to assign users to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 543 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/543). If omitted, assigns to the issue that triggered this workflow.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: assign_to_user

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Post a routing comment @mentioning team owners on the triggering issue; bypasses safe-outputs mention neutralization

message.description: The comment body text without any @mentions or @ symbols

owners.description: Comma-separated GitHub usernames to notify, without the @ prefix (e.g. 'user1, user2, Azure/team-name')

tools.name: mention_owners

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

content.text: {"number":58272,"title":"[GeneratorAgent] Docs: Add MPG migration Copilot Chat example to README","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `Azure.GeneratorAgent` **Service directory:** `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` **Triggered by:** [REDACTED] (#58169) by `@live1206` ## What Changed PR #58169 separated the previously unified `sdk-migration` skill into two distinct skills: - `.github/skills/dpg-migration/SKILL.md` — data-plane migration workflow - `.github/skills/mpg-migration/SKILL.md` — management-plane migration workflow (newly added) The `README.md` was updated to reference both skills throughout, but the **Examples** section's "Use with Copilot Chat" subsection was not updated to include an MPG migration example. ## Gaps Found - The **`Examples → Use with Copilot Chat`** section only shows how to invoke `dpg-migration` via Copilot Chat prompt, with no equivalent example for `mpg-migration` - The MPG migration skill has different required inputs (targets `Azure.ResourceManager.*` packages; spec repo path is optional since it defaults to `../azure-rest-api-specs`) and the prompt pattern should be documented in parallel with the DPG example - The "Use with a migration skill" subsection describes a generic workflow that pre-dates the split; it could call out the setup-phase difference between DPG and MPG (MPG uses `RegenSdkLocal.ps1` rather than `dotnet build /t:GenerateCode`) 📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files **File:** `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/README.md` — **edit** In the `## Examples` section, the `### Use with Copilot Chat` subsection currently ends after the DPG example. Add an MPG migration subsection immediately after the closing paragraph of the DPG section: ````markdown For management-plane libraries (`Azure.ResourceManager.*`), use the `mpg-migration` skill: ``` Use the mpg-migration skill to migrate (repo-root)/sdk/(service)/Azure.ResourceManager.(Service) The local specs repo is at (specs-root)/specification/(service)/(spec-directory) ``` For example: ``` Use the mpg-migration skill to migrate C:\git\azure-sdk-for-net\sdk\compute\Azure.ResourceManager.Compute The local specs repo is at C:\git\azure-rest-api-specs\specification\compute\resource-manager ``` If the specs repo is already at the default sibling path (`../azure-rest-api-specs`), you can omit the path: ``` Use the mpg-migration skill to migrate C:\git\azure-sdk-for-net\sdk\compute\Azure.ResourceManager.Compute ``` ```` Also update the `### Use with a migration skill` subsection to briefly note the setup-phase difference between DPG and MPG. Replace the current generic workflow list: ```markdown The tools are designed to be called by the dedicated migration skills. Use [`dpg-migration`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/skills/dpg-migration/SKILL.md) for data-plane libraries and [`mpg-migration`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/skills/mpg-migration/SKILL.md) for management-plane libraries. The workflows share the same build-fix loop but differ in setup: **DPG (data-plane) workflow:** 1. **Setup** — `pregen_cleanup` → `validate_tsp_config` → `commit_iteration` → `run_code_generation` 2. **Build-fix loop** — `build_and_classify` → `batch_fix` (for deterministic errors) → LLM reasoning (for non-deterministic errors) → rebuild 3. **Finalize** — `migrate_test_samples` → `run_tests` → `finalize_migration` **MPG (management-plane) workflow:** 1. **Setup** — Update `tsp-location.yaml` → `run_code_generation` (or `RegenSdkLocal.ps1`) 2. **Build-fix loop** — `build_and_classify` → `batch_fix` (deterministic) → spec-side decorator fixes → rebuild 3. **Breaking-change mitigation** — Export API → `batch_fix` → re-export → validate 4. **Finalize** — `finalize_migration` → `run_tests` ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No snippet changes required — this is pure prose documentation. ### Step 3: Verify README structure The README at `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/README.md` already contains all required sections in the correct order: 1. ✅ Getting started (Install/Build, Prerequisites, Authenticate the client) 2. ✅ Key concepts 3. ⚠️ **Examples** — "Use with Copilot Chat" subsection is missing MPG example (this issue) 4. ✅ Troubleshooting 5. ✅ Next steps 6. ✅ Contributing ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order: 1. `dotnet build sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 tools` — confirm no unexpected README changes 4. `dotnet format sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24554809637/agentic_workflow) · ● 480.6K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/58272","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-17T08:13:47Z","updated_at":"2026-04-17T08:13:47Z"}

Add an MPG migration subsection immediately after the closing paragraph of the DPG section: ````markdown For management-plane libraries (`Azure.ResourceManager.*`), use the `mpg-migration` skill: ``` Use the mpg-migration skill to migrate (repo-root)/sdk/(service)/Azure.ResourceManager.(Service) The local specs repo is at (specs-root)/specification/(service)/(spec-directory) ``` For example: ``` Use the mpg-migration skill to migrate C:\git\azure-sdk-for-net\sdk\compute\Azure.ResourceManager.Compute The local specs repo is at C:\git\azure-rest-api-specs\specification\compute\resource-manager ``` If the specs repo is already at the default sibling path (`../azure-rest-api-specs`), you can omit the path: ``` Use the mpg-migration skill to migrate C:\git\azure-sdk-for-net\sdk\compute\Azure.ResourceManager.Compute ``` ```` Also update the `### Use with a migration skill` subsection to briefly note the setup-phase difference between DPG and MPG. Use [`dpg-migration`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/skills/dpg-migration/SKILL.md) for data-plane libraries and [`mpg-migration`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/skills/mpg-migration/SKILL.md) for management-plane libraries. The workflows share the same build-fix loop but differ in setup: **DPG (data-plane) workflow:** 1. **Setup** — `pregen_cleanup` → `validate_tsp_config` → `commit_iteration` → `run_code_generation` 2. **Build-fix loop** — `build_and_classify` → `batch_fix` (for deterministic errors) → LLM reasoning (for non-deterministic errors) → rebuild 3. **Finalize** — `migrate_test_samples` → `run_tests` → `finalize_migration` **MPG (management-plane) workflow:** 1. **Setup** — Update `tsp-location.yaml` → `run_code_generation` (or `RegenSdkLocal.ps1`) 2. **Build-fix loop** — `build_and_classify` → `batch_fix` (deterministic) → spec-side decorator fixes → rebuild 3. **Breaking-change mitigation** — Export API → `batch_fix` → re-export → validate 4. **Finalize** — `finalize_migration` → `run_tests` ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No snippet changes required — this is pure prose documentation. ### Step 3: Verify README structure The README at `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/README.md` already contains all required sections in the correct order: 1. ✅ Getting started (Install/Build, Prerequisites, Authenticate the client) 2. ✅ Key concepts 3. ⚠️ **Examples** — "Use with Copilot Chat" subsection is missing MPG example (this issue) 4. ✅ Troubleshooting 5. ✅ Next steps 6. ✅ Contributing ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order: 1. `dotnet build sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 tools` — confirm no unexpected README changes 4. `dotnet format sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24554809637/agentic_workflow) · ● 480.6K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/58272","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-17T08:13:47Z","updated_at":"2026-04-17T08:13:47Z"}

arguments.body: Thank you for your feedback. Tagging and routing to the team member(s) best able to assist.

## 🎯 Agentic Issue Triage

**Summary:** This bot-filed issue identifies a documentation gap in `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/README.md` — the "Examples → Use with Copilot Chat" section is missing an MPG migration example after PR #58169 split the unified `sdk-migration` skill into separate `dpg-migration` and `mpg-migration` skills.

<details>
<summary>📋 Issue Details</summary>

**Package:** `Azure.GeneratorAgent` (`sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/`)
**Affected API:** `README.md` — `## Examples → ### Use with Copilot Chat` and `### Use with a migration skill` subsections
**Scenarios:**
- No Copilot Chat prompt example exists for invoking `mpg-migration` (only `dpg-migration` is shown)
- The `### Use with a migration skill` subsection describes a generic pre-split workflow; it doesn't distinguish DPG vs. MPG setup steps (e.g., MPG uses `RegenSdkLocal.ps1` rather than `dotnet build /t:GenerateCode`)

**Root ask:** Add an MPG migration Copilot Chat prompt example to the README in parallel with the existing DPG example, and update the migration workflow description to call out the DPG vs. MPG setup differences.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🔎 Debugging / Reproduction Notes</summary>

This is a pure documentation issue triggered automatically by commit fea03a2e (PR #58169). No runtime bug or API failure is involved.

**Suggested investigation steps:**
1. Open `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/README.md` and locate the `### Use with Copilot Chat` subsection under `## Examples`
2. Confirm the MPG example block (for `Azure.ResourceManager.*` packages) is absent
3. Add the MPG prompt example and optional-specs-path variant as described in the issue's Implementation Guide
4. Update `### Use with a migration skill` to distinguish DPG and MPG workflows
5. Run `dotnet build sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` and `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 tools` to confirm no unintended changes
</details>

<details>
<summary>🏷️ Label Confidence</summary>

- **Category:** `Client` — `Azure.GeneratorAgent` is a non-management SDK tool (not under `Azure.ResourceManager.*` or `Azure.Provisioning.*`); the issue is about SDK tooling behavior/docs
- **Service:** `CodeGen` — the package lives under `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` which is associated with `# PRLabel: %CodeGen` in CODEOWNERS (line 1203); reference issue #23940 (`Client` + `CodeGen`) confirms this pattern for code-generation tooling
- **Confidence:** High — the package is explicitly named, maps unambiguously to a single service directory, and the `Client` + `CodeGen` combination is validated by a quality reference issue
</details>

<details>
<summary>👥 Owner Routing</summary>

- **Matched CODEOWNERS entry:** `# ServiceLabel: %CodeGen` (line 1218) — the predicted service label `CodeGen` matches; only `%CodeGen` is required by this entry, and the issue carries that label
- **AzureSdkOwners:** @JoshLove-msft
- **ServiceOwners:** none listed
- **Routing action:** Assigned `@JoshLove-msft` (single AzureSdkOwner); `needs-team-attention` not added because this is a bot-filed issue (no `customer-reported` label)
- **Scan notes:** `# ServiceLabel: %CodeGen %Mgmt` (line 1221) was encountered first (bottom-to-top) but requires both `%CodeGen` AND `%Mgmt`; the issue has `Client` not `Mgmt`, so it was skipped
</details>
